quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Newton's First Law"?

options.text: KE_rot = ½Iω²; energy due to rotation, analogous to ½mv² for translation
An object remains at rest or moves at constant velocity unless acted upon by a net external force (the law of inertia)
F = Gm₁m₂/r²; every pair of masses attracts each other with a force proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance
τ = -mgd sinθ for a pendulum; for small θ, τ ≈ -mgdθ, giving angular SHM with ω = √(mgd/I)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Newton's First Law" is defined as: An object remains at rest or moves at constant velocity unless acted upon by a net external force (the law of inertia). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Newton's Second Law"?

options.text: τ = -mgd sinθ for a pendulum; for small θ, τ ≈ -mgdθ, giving angular SHM with ω = √(mgd/I)
F_net = ma or F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals mass times acceleration, or equivalently the rate of change of momentum
ω = √(k/m) = 2πf = 2π/T; determines how fast the oscillation occurs
Oscillation where F = -kx and x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ); characterized by constant period independent of amplitude

quiz_questions.explanation: "Newton's Second Law" is defined as: F_net = ma or F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals mass times acceleration, or equivalently the rate of change of momentum. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Newton's Third Law"?

options.text: For every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force acting on a different object; forces come in pairs
W = ∫F·dr; the scalar integral of force along displacement; measured in joules (J); positive when force and displacement are in the same direction
f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N
When two waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference and standing waves

quiz_questions.explanation: "Newton's Third Law" is defined as: For every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force acting on a different object; forces come in pairs. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Free-body diagram"?

options.text: KE_rot = ½Iω²; energy due to rotation, analogous to ½mv² for translation
p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity, measured in kg·m/s
A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity
A diagram showing all external forces acting on an object, drawn as vectors from the object's center of mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Free-body diagram" is defined as: A diagram showing all external forces acting on an object, drawn as vectors from the object's center of mass. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Net force"?

options.text: The gravitational force W = mg directed toward Earth's center; a body force, not a contact force
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma
W_net = ½mv² - ½mv₀²; relates the total work done by all forces to the change in kinetic energy
f_k = μ_k N; the friction force during sliding, proportional to the normal force; typically μ_k < μ_s

quiz_questions.explanation: "Net force" is defined as: The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An object remains at rest or moves at constant velocity unless acted upon by a net external force (the law of inertia)"?

options.text: Restoring torque
Newton's First Law
Rotational kinematic equations
Energy in pendulum

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Newton's First Law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F_net = ma or F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals mass times acceleration, or equivalently the rate of change of momentum"?

options.text: Coefficient of friction
Angular momentum (particle)
Total orbital energy
Newton's Second Law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Newton's Second Law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "For every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force acting on a different object; forces come in pairs"?

options.text: Linear momentum
Total energy in orbit
Newton's Third Law
Uniform circular motion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Newton's Third Law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A diagram showing all external forces acting on an object, drawn as vectors from the object's center of mass"?

options.text: Free-body diagram
Energy conservation in orbits
Angular impulse
Coefficient of restitution

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Free-body diagram".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma"?

options.text: Apparent weight
Net force
Coefficient of restitution
Instantaneous power

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Net force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Dynamics is accurate?

options.text: The parallel axis theorem I = I_cm + Md² relates the moment of inertia about any axis to that about the center of mass.
Conservative Forces and Potential Energy connect force to potential energy through F = -dU/dr (in one dimension) or F = -∇U (in three dimensions).
When non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance) are present, the generalized equation becomes KE₁ + PE₁ + W_nc = KE₂ + PE₂, where W_nc represents energy converted to or from thermal energy.
Dynamics connects force and motion through Newton's three laws.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Dynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Dynamics?

options.text: The Work-Energy Theorem (W_net = ΔKE) provides a powerful alternative to Newton's Second Law for solving problems.
Setting gravitational force equal to centripetal force: GMm/r² = mv²/r gives orbital speed v = √(GM/r) and period T = 2π√(r³/GM), which is Kepler's Third Law.
Friction problems often involve inclined planes, circular motion, and connected bodies.
Newton's Second Law, F_net = ma (or more precisely F_net = dp/dt), relates the net force on an object to its acceleration.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Dynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Dynamics?

options.text: Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) occurs when the restoring force is proportional to displacement: F = -kx, producing sinusoidal oscillations x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ).
The normal force N adjusts to maintain contact and is not always equal to mg—it depends on the geometry and other applied forces.
Free-body diagrams identify all forces acting on an object, enabling systematic application of F = ma in each coordinate direction.
A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Dynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Dynamics?

options.text: Dynamics problems in AP Physics C require setting up and solving differential equations when forces depend on velocity or position.

Energy conservation with U = -GMm/r is essential for orbit analysis: total mechanical energy E = ½mv² - GMm/r determines whether an orbit is bound (E < 0) or unbound (E ≥ 0), and escape velocity follows from setting E = 0.
Newton's Second Law for rotation in momentum form gives τ_net = dL/dt.
Conservation of angular momentum explains phenomena from spinning ice skaters to the formation of galaxies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Dynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Dynamics?

options.text: Dynamics connects force and motion through Newton's three laws.
Real collisions are partially inelastic.
Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension describes the motion of objects along a straight line using position x(t), velocity v(t) = dx/dt, and acceleration a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt².
The normal force N adjusts to maintain contact and is not always equal to mg—it depends on the geometry and other applied forces.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Dynamics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Dynamics?

options.text: Structural engineering calculating forces on bridge components using free-body diagrams and Newton's laws to ensure safe load-bearing capacity
Earthquake-resistant building design using tuned mass dampers (large SHM systems) to counteract the building's oscillation and reduce structural damage
Interplanetary mission design computing escape velocity from Earth and transfer orbits (Hohmann transfers) to reach Mars or other planets
Highway engineering designing banked curves at optimal angles so vehicles can navigate turns safely at design speed without relying on friction

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Dynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Dynamics?

options.text: Automotive crash testing measuring force-time profiles with accelerometers to calculate impulse and evaluate occupant protection systems
Athletic performance analysis measuring power output of cyclists and runners to optimize training and predict race performance
Automotive engineering designing braking systems by analyzing deceleration forces and stopping distances using F = ma
Computer graphics and game physics engines using dot and cross products for collision detection, lighting calculations, and surface normal computations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Dynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Dynamics applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Athletic performance analysis measuring power output of cyclists and runners to optimize training and predict race performance
Spacecraft trajectory planning using Newton's Second Law with thrust force, gravitational force, and changing mass to compute acceleration profiles
Hard drive engineering specifying angular velocity (7200 RPM) and computing read/write head linear speed at different radii
Analyzing inclined conveyor belt forces in mining operations to determine motor power needed to transport ore uphill

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Dynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Dynamics?

options.text: Quartz crystal oscillators in watches using the precise SHM frequency of a quartz crystal (32,768 Hz) for accurate timekeeping
Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Calculating the work done by a non-constant wind force on a sail using numerical integration of force-displacement data
Structural engineering calculating forces on bridge components using free-body diagrams and Newton's laws to ensure safe load-bearing capacity

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Dynamics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Dynamics in practice?

options.text: SpaceX and NASA calculating orbital insertion burns using v = √(GM/r) to place satellites at specific altitudes and orbital velocities
Automotive engineering designing braking systems by analyzing deceleration forces and stopping distances using F = ma
CD/DVD drive technology computing read speeds at different radii using ω = v/r to maintain constant linear velocity as the read head moves
Analyzing the work required to stretch a bungee cord by integrating the non-linear elastic force over the extension distance

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Dynamics.
